articles.title: Why Energy Bills Might Go Up
articles.summary: The boss of British Gas, Chris O'Shea, says that if oil prices stay high because of a war involving Iran, energy bills for homes in the UK will likely go up. He explains that the war has closed an important waterway called the Strait of Hormuz, which stops a lot of oil from moving around the world. This makes oil prices rise, and that can affect how much we pay for energy. However, he thinks gas prices might not go up as much because less gas is affected. The government is talking about ways to help people with their bills, like giving money to those who need it most. A forecast says bills could rise by about £332 from July, but it's still early to know for sure. The boss suggests that instead of helping everyone, the government should focus on those who really need support.

articles.title: A Police Officer's Sad Crash
articles.summary: A police officer named Bradley Corke died in a car crash while he was driving to help people in an emergency. He was 27 years old and worked for Kent Police. The crash happened on a road called A20 in Farningham, near Swanley, at about 7:45 PM. Another police officer in the same car is very sick in the hospital, and a person in another car is also hurt. Bradley's family and friends are very sad. People say he was brave and loved helping others. The police are asking anyone who saw the crash to tell them what happened. This shows that being a police officer can be dangerous because they risk their lives to keep us safe.

articles.title: Iranian Missiles Hurt People Near Israeli Nuclear Site
articles.summary: Iran fired missiles at two towns in Israel near a nuclear site, injuring over 160 people, including many children. The attacks happened on Saturday evening in Arad and Dimona, causing serious damage to buildings and leaving craters. Emergency workers helped injured people, like a 10-year-old girl with head injuries from broken glass. Iran said it was responding to an earlier attack on its own nuclear facility. Israel says most missiles were stopped, but some got through. No damage was reported at the nuclear research center nearby. This conflict shows how missile strikes can hurt civilians and create fear in communities.

articles.title: Pop Star Stops Show for Safety
articles.summary: A pop star named Sombr stopped his concert in London because he saw a fan who had passed out. He was worried about safety and said the venue was poorly managed. The venue said staff helped the fan quickly, and the fan was okay. This concert happened at Brixton Academy, a place where two people died in a crowd crush in 2022. Other singers like Billie Eilish and Harry Styles have also stopped shows to help fans. Experts say artists care more about safety after a festival where 10 people died in 2021. The article asks if concerts in the UK are really risky.

articles.title: Checking Facts About a New Voting Law
articles.summary: This article checks what a politician said about a new voting law called the SAVE America Act. Senate leader Chuck Schumer said the law would make people register to vote only in person, which he said only 5% of Americans do. The article says Schumer is right that the law requires people to show proof of citizenship in person to register. But he is wrong about the percentage; federal data shows more than 5% of Americans register in person. The law is supported by President Trump and passed in the House. It aims to prevent noncitizens from voting, which is very rare. Experts say the law could affect many people, like those who move to a new county, because they might need to show citizenship proof again. The law is unclear about who counts as an election official, which could cause confusion.

articles.title: Missile Attack in Israel Hurts People Near Nuclear Center
articles.summary: Iran fired missiles at two towns in Israel near a big nuclear research center. This happened late on a Saturday. At least seven people were badly hurt, and many buildings were damaged. Israel's leader said it was a hard evening and sent more help. This is the first time the nuclear center has been hit in the fighting. The war started a few weeks ago and is not close to ending. Iran also hit a faraway military base, showing its missiles can go very far. The war is making food and fuel prices go up around the world. Rescue workers helped people from damaged buildings, and many went to hospitals.
